quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cladogram"?

options.text: Using the exergonic hydrolysis of ATP to drive an endergonic reaction, often through phosphorylation of a substrate
The totality of an organism's chemical reactions; catabolism (breakdown, releases energy) and anabolism (synthesis, requires energy)
A branching diagram showing evolutionary relationships based on shared derived characters (synapomorphies); branch lengths may or may not represent time
Two rounds of nuclear division producing four haploid gametes (2n → n); includes crossing over and independent assortment for genetic diversity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cladogram" is defined as: A branching diagram showing evolutionary relationships based on shared derived characters (synapomorphies); branch lengths may or may not represent time. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Monophyletic group (clade)"?

options.text: Normal cells stop dividing when they contact neighboring cells; cancer cells lose this control (contact inhibition)
A group consisting of a common ancestor and ALL of its descendants; the fundamental unit in modern classification
Changes in chromatin structure that affect gene accessibility: histone acetylation opens chromatin (activating), deacetylation compacts it (silencing)
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase; the most abundant enzyme on Earth; fixes CO₂ but also has oxygenase activity (photorespiration)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Monophyletic group (clade)" is defined as: A group consisting of a common ancestor and ALL of its descendants; the fundamental unit in modern classification. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Synapomorphy"?

options.text: Membrane-bound organelle containing chromatin (DNA + histones); site of DNA replication, transcription, and ribosome assembly (nucleolus)
Random orientation of homologous pairs at metaphase I, producing 2ⁿ possible gamete combinations (n = haploid number)
A shared derived character that defines a clade; evidence of common ancestry (e.g., feathers in birds)
The specific region of an enzyme where the substrate binds and the catalytic reaction occurs; shape determines specificity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Synapomorphy" is defined as: A shared derived character that defines a clade; evidence of common ancestry (e.g., feathers in birds). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Outgroup"?

options.text: Speciation driven by geographic isolation that prevents gene flow between populations, allowing independent evolution
A species or group outside the clade being studied, used to root the phylogenetic tree and determine which characters are ancestral vs. derived
No mutation, no migration, random mating, no selection, large population size; violations drive evolution
Control points at G1, G2, and M where the cell evaluates conditions before proceeding; can halt the cycle if conditions are unfavorable

quiz_questions.explanation: "Outgroup" is defined as: A species or group outside the clade being studied, used to root the phylogenetic tree and determine which characters are ancestral vs. derived. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Molecular clock"?

options.text: Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., Iᴬ Iᴮ blood type produces both A and B antigens)
Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap
The concept that DNA sequences accumulate mutations at a roughly constant rate, allowing estimation of divergence times between lineages
Two rounds of nuclear division producing four haploid gametes (2n → n); includes crossing over and independent assortment for genetic diversity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Molecular clock" is defined as: The concept that DNA sequences accumulate mutations at a roughly constant rate, allowing estimation of divergence times between lineages. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A branching diagram showing evolutionary relationships based on shared derived characters (synapomorphies); branch lengths may or may not represent time"?

options.text: Cladogram
Mitosis
Energy coupling
Cohesion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cladogram".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A group consisting of a common ancestor and ALL of its descendants; the fundamental unit in modern classification"?

options.text: Monophyletic group (clade)
Denaturation
Chitin
Keystone species

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Monophyletic group (clade)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A shared derived character that defines a clade; evidence of common ancestry (e.g., feathers in birds)"?

options.text: Exponential growth
Synapomorphy
Tertiary structure
Gel electrophoresis

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Synapomorphy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A species or group outside the clade being studied, used to root the phylogenetic tree and determine which characters are ancestral vs. derived"?

options.text: Outgroup
Species-area relationship
Cohesion
Latitudinal diversity gradient

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Outgroup".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The concept that DNA sequences accumulate mutations at a roughly constant rate, allowing estimation of divergence times between lineages"?

options.text: Molecular clock
Cellular respiration (overall)
Frameshift mutation
Nonsense mutation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Molecular clock".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Phylogenetics is accurate?

options.text: Phylogenetics reconstructs evolutionary relationships using shared derived characters (synapomorphies) organized in cladograms or phylogenetic trees.
Current extinction rates are 100-1,000 times higher than background rates due to habitat destruction, invasive species, overexploitation, pollution, and climate change (HIPPO threats).
Water is split (photolysis) at PSII, releasing O₂.
When damage is too severe, p53 activates apoptosis to prevent propagation of mutations that could lead to cancer.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Phylogenetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Phylogenetics?

options.text: Translation is the process by which ribosomes synthesize proteins from mRNA templates.
Molecular phylogenetics compares DNA, RNA, or protein sequences to build trees based on sequence similarity.
Enzyme activity follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics characterized by Vmax (maximum velocity) and Km (substrate concentration at half Vmax).
NADH and FADH₂ donate electrons to protein complexes (I-IV), which pump H⁺ from the matrix to the intermembrane space, creating a proton gradient.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Phylogenetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Phylogenetics?

options.text: While most mutations are neutral or harmful, some confer advantages (e.g., sickle cell heterozygote advantage against malaria).
Mutations can be spontaneous (replication errors) or induced by mutagens.
Parsimony (fewest evolutionary changes) and maximum likelihood methods are used to determine the most probable tree topology.
Adaptive radiation (rapid diversification from a single ancestor into multiple ecological niches) often follows mass extinctions or colonization of new environments.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Phylogenetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Phylogenetics?

options.text: Natural selection acts on phenotypic variation, favoring traits that increase fitness (reproductive success) in a specific environment.
Phylogenies reveal the nested hierarchy of life and enable predictions about uncharacterized organisms.
Evolution and Evidence establishes that populations change over time through multiple converging lines of evidence.
The overall equation is C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ~30-32 ATP.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Phylogenetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Phylogenetics?

options.text: Phospholipids form the basis of cell membrane bilayers due to their amphipathic nature (hydrophilic head, hydrophobic tails).

The three stages—reception (ligand binding), transduction (signal relay/amplification through phosphorylation cascades), and response (gene expression, enzyme activation, or cell movement)—allow cells to respond to their environment.
Nutrients (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water) cycle between biotic and abiotic components.
Phylogenetics reconstructs evolutionary relationships using shared derived characters (synapomorphies) organized in cladograms or phylogenetic trees.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Phylogenetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Phylogenetics?

options.text: Ecosystem services valuation estimating that pollination by insects is worth $235-577 billion annually globally, providing economic justification for pollinator conservation
Using molecular phylogenetics to trace the origin and spread of HIV, determining it crossed from chimpanzees to humans around 1920 in central Africa
Metabolic interconnections: Krebs cycle intermediates serve as precursors for amino acid synthesis, fatty acid synthesis, and gluconeogenesis
Human population growth following a roughly exponential pattern from 1 billion (1800) to 8 billion (2022), with demographic transition slowing growth in developed nations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Phylogenetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Phylogenetics?

options.text: Aspirin acting as an irreversible inhibitor of cyclooxygenase (COX), blocking prostaglandin synthesis and reducing pain, inflammation, and blood clotting

Wolf reintroduction into Yellowstone (1995) demonstrating trophic cascades: wolves reduced elk overgrazing, allowing riparian vegetation recovery, which stabilized stream banks and increased beaver populations
Phylogenomic analysis revealing that birds are living dinosaurs (nested within theropod dinosaurs), fundamentally changing our classification of vertebrates
Genetic engineering using the universal genetic code to express human insulin in E. coli bacteria, producing recombinant insulin for diabetes treatment

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Phylogenetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Phylogenetics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: CRISPR-Cas9 clinical trials treating sickle cell disease by editing the BCL11A gene in patient stem cells to reactivate fetal hemoglobin production
Artificial selection in agriculture: selective breeding transforming teosinte (small, hard kernels) into modern corn over thousands of years of human-directed selection

Epinephrine (adrenaline) signaling cascade: one epinephrine molecule activates many G-proteins, each activating adenylyl cyclase producing millions of cAMP molecules, amplifying the fight-or-flight response
Virus tracking during COVID-19 pandemic using SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing to build phylogenetic trees tracing transmission chains and identifying new variants

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Phylogenetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Phylogenetics?

options.text: The Human Genome Project using genetic and physical mapping to determine the positions of all ~20,000 human genes across 23 chromosome pairs
Xeroderma pigmentosum caused by defective nucleotide excision repair, making patients extremely sensitive to UV radiation-induced DNA damage
Using molecular phylogenetics to trace the origin and spread of HIV, determining it crossed from chimpanzees to humans around 1920 in central Africa
Agricultural selective breeding applying Mendelian genetics to develop crop varieties with desired traits (disease resistance, yield) over multiple generations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Phylogenetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Phylogenetics in practice?

options.text: Feedback inhibition in the cholesterol synthesis pathway: statins mimic the substrate of HMG-CoA reductase, acting as competitive inhibitors to lower cholesterol levels
DNA profiling (forensic fingerprinting) using short tandem repeat (STR) polymorphisms for criminal identification with error rates below 1 in a billion
Phylogenomic analysis revealing that birds are living dinosaurs (nested within theropod dinosaurs), fundamentally changing our classification of vertebrates
Galápagos finch adaptive radiation: a single ancestor species diversifying into 14 species with specialized beak shapes for different food sources within ~1-2 million years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Phylogenetics.
